items.reality: It's still single-threaded; await yields control so other work runs while I/O completes in the background — concurrency, not parallel threads.
items.example:
```ts
async function spin() { while (true) {} }   // blocks the event loop
spin()                                       // never yields; no await
await fetch('/')                              // never runs — single thread is busy
```

items.reality: It only pauses the current async function; the event loop keeps handling other tasks meanwhile.
items.example: An HTTP handler `await db.query()` doesn't stop other requests. The event loop services other handlers; this one resumes when the query resolves.

items.myth: A Promise can resolve more than once.
items.reality: A Promise settles exactly once (fulfilled or rejected) and then never changes state again.
items.example:
```ts
const p = new Promise(res => { res(1); res(2) })
p.then(console.log)   // 1   ← second res() is ignored
```
The Promise is settled at the first resolve; subsequent attempts are no-ops.
